headline: Three Investigations on Chinese Platforms — and What They Don't Prove
context: WeChat, TikTok and RedNote operate under PRC content rules. Malaysia licensed the first two in Jan 2025 but the Online Safety Act 2025 excludes private messaging. Joint Merdeka/UM survey: Chinese-Malaysian favourability of China rose 41% to 67%; Malay favourability fell 33% to 28% (2016 to 2022).

cards.big: Three different investigations point at three different things about Chinese-owned platforms — and bundling them produces a story stronger than the evidence.
cards.sub: Citizen Lab, Sinopsis and ISEAS document three separate findings, not one. The shared thread is opacity — not what readers believe.

cards.big: WeChat surveils images and documents shared between non-China accounts to train its China-side censorship system
cards.sub: Citizen Lab documented this in 2020. The same paper is explicit it could not test chat-text between non-China accounts and found no evidence linking it to CCP direction.

cards.big: Malaysia licensed WeChat and TikTok in Jan 2025 — but the Online Safety Act 2025 excludes private messaging from its scope
cards.sub: Licences regulate presence. The Act regulates harmful content but not private messages. No public instrument requires platforms to disclose moderation algorithms to MCMC.

cards.big: Chinese-Malaysian favourability of China rose 41% to 67%; Malay favourability fell 33% to 28% (2016 to 2022)
cards.sub: Joint Merdeka/UM survey via RFA and Oriental Daily. The divergent trajectories rule out a single-platform story. UKM/ANU documented Malay-language TikTok disinfo around the 2022 election.

cards.big: The question is not which Malaysian community believes Beijing. It is whether any user — Chinese, Malay, Tamil, English — sees how their platform decides what news reaches them.
Think of it like a landlord who decides which tenants' mail gets delivered
cards.sub: The landlord writes the rules, the rules are not public, and Malaysia's regulator licenses the landlord without seeing the rules. WeChat, TikTok, RedNote, Facebook, Telegram are all landlords.

cards.big: PRC platforms carry a legal duty US and EU platforms do not. Malaysia regulates platform presence, not moderation logic
cards.sub: Name the asymmetry (PRC National Intelligence Law Art 7); apply transparency universally — closing the WeChat gap also exposes the Malay-language TikTok disinfo around 2022.
